chart_selection.rule: Choose a chart by the relationship in the data, not by what looks impressive.

by_relationship.avoid: Pie chart for more than 5 segments, 3D charts
by_relationship.rule: Horizontal bars when labels are long. Vertical bars for time series with few periods.

by_relationship.best: Line chart for multiple series, area chart for single series showing volume
by_relationship.avoid: Bar chart for time series with many data points — becomes a wall
by_relationship.rule: Always start y-axis at 0 for absolute values. Truncated y-axis only for percentage-change charts.

by_relationship.best: Donut chart for 2–5 segments with clear labels. Stacked bar for showing composition change over time.
by_relationship.avoid: Pie charts with more than 5 slices — use a bar chart instead
by_relationship.rule: Always label percentages directly on slices, not just in legend.

by_relationship.best: Histogram for frequency distribution. Box plot for showing outliers.
by_relationship.avoid: Average alone — it hides distribution. Always pair with standard deviation or range.
by_relationship.rule: Consumer products rarely need distribution charts. B2B/analytics products: prefer them over averages alone.

chart_types: Bubble chart (adds third variable as size)
by_relationship.best: Scatter plot — simplest and most honest
by_relationship.rule: Add a regression/trend line if correlation is meaningful. Label outliers explicitly.

by_relationship.best: Choropleth for regional comparison. Dot map for individual locations.
by_relationship.rule: Never use 3D maps — they distort perception by perspective.

by_relationship.best: Funnel chart for conversion stages. Sankey for multi-path flows.
by_relationship.rule: Funnel charts: show actual numbers AND percentages. Drop-off % between each stage.

rules: NEVER use red and green as the only differentiators — 8% of males are red-green colorblind
Use shape + pattern in addition to color for any categorical distinction
Sequential data (low→high intensity): single-hue scale (light to dark of one color)
Diverging data (negative to positive): two-hue scale from neutral center (e.g., red→white→blue)
Categorical data (unordered groups): distinct hue palette (max 7 colors — more than 7 and users can't track)
Highlight a single data point: use accent color, keep everything else neutral gray
Data colors are separate from UI colors — never reuse --accent for chart bars

colorblind_safe_palette.description: Okabe-Ito palette — distinguishable by all forms of color vision deficiency
colorblind_safe_palette.rule: Use this palette as default for categorical charts. Never use pure red (#FF0000) or pure green (#00FF00) as chart colors.

axis_and_labels: Y-axis labels: left-aligned, horizontal — never rotated y-axis labels
X-axis labels: rotate 45° only if labels are too long to fit horizontally
Grid lines: light gray (--border-muted), horizontal only for bar/line charts, minimal density (4–6 lines)
Data labels: show on hover tooltip by default. Static labels only for small datasets (≤10 points) or key highlights.
Legend: place above or below chart, never inside data area. Clicking legend item should toggle series visibility.
Zero line: always visible if chart includes negative values. Bold the zero line.
Truncation warning: if y-axis doesn't start at 0, add a visual break indicator and annotate clearly

metric_cards.anatomy: Primary number (large, font-bold) → label (text-secondary, text-sm) → trend indicator (delta % with arrow + semantic color)
metric_cards.rule: Always show the context — '23% vs last month' not just '23%'. Numbers without context mean nothing.

sparklines.use: Inline trend visualization inside metric cards — no axes, no labels, just the shape of the trend
sparklines.color: Use semantic color matching trend direction (success green for up, danger red for down) or just accent
sparklines.rule: Sparklines are supplementary — never the only trend indicator. Pair with percentage label.

rules: Simplify before you visualize — if a table communicates better than a chart, use a table
One insight per chart — if you need to explain a chart, it needs to be simpler
Mobile: stack charts vertically, reduce data series to 1–2, use shorter time ranges
Provide accessible text alternative for every chart (summary table or aria-label with key finding)
Animate chart entrance in 300–400ms ease-out. Data updates: cross-fade or morph, not rebuild.
